Delivery organization.

So you'll be working...
With a director, oh, there's also an account strategist.

We improve prompts, process, things like that to put out like really high quality content for our clients and making sure that we're like meeting our contractual obligations.

And UMA.

And UMA is a decentralized oracle project.
And I handled the content function for both of those projects under the Risk Lab umbrella.
I left Risk Labs in July because they were restructuring the content function and they had to think about where I wanted to do next more intentionally.
Before Risk Labs, I was at Cryptonary, where I was the first head of editorial and later head of content.
And Cryptonary is more like a finance research organization.
The main product at Cryptonary was content because we were basically selling time-sensitive reports that people then make decisions based on.
And it was really, it has to be very reflexive.
So at Risk Labs, would say my role was more of content operations.

Storytelling and strategic, how would I call it, managing strategic stakeholders, because the engineers across those projects, the projects were actually built originally by engineers and the marketing department was new.

But at Criptonary, the role was a bit different.
was more like turning this team of web career enthusiasts into a disciplined editorial machine, so to say.
So before I joined, they would just publish whatever they feel like or whenever.
But you can't do that because people are actually paying money.
It's a paid service, not a premium.
So people pay money, they expect a cadence, they expect a system of days.
So it's really bringing that sense of discipline, sort of function, creating checklists, style guides, and stuff like that.
At Criptonary, before I then became the head of content, we then went beyond just the written content to doing stuff like this.

videos, and creating courses, and stuff like that, before, before Cryptonary, I was previously at Animals, so I would say Animals is really where I developed the fundamentals of scaling content, because I was having like four different clients, and they have deliverables at the same time, in the same way, and you have to keep juggling all of those books, so I would say that's my experience, so Usman, an ex-colleague from Animals, reached out to me about the growthx opportunity, actually.

Well, can you tell me a little bit about the process of bringing more structure to Cryptonary, and how you approached it from a program management standpoint, what steps did you take, or, if you have another example example of like...
Managing a project like that.
So I think I can go from Kryptonara and even come back to Risk Labs, because like I said, there was no marketing department when we were at Risk Labs.
But Kryptonara, so they had a bunch of freelancers, so to say, and then they had another set of contributors that were more like on a contract basis, but they were not employees who delivered pieces.
The thing is, those people basically spend time in the crypto market, and when they feel like they have something to say, they say it.
And then it's a bunch of people, so it's more like Kryptonara felt more like a blog of just disconnected people, rather than a publishing house.
In that sense of the way it was, the boxes were disparate, it didn't feel like a corporate structure.
So my job when I came in was to actually organize the whole thing.
So we need to come on a full-time retainer, even if it's like a contractor contract, we'll stay as a freelancer.
We can cover multiple bits, like do we really need poor freelancers for four different topics?
Can we actually...
We have somebody to cover, maybe three at the same time concurrently, and have them on them all.
So setting out those workflows in terms of who does what, then, okay, let's now design, how do we speak as cryptocurrency?
So it's a UK company, but we're speaking to people from all over the world.
So are we speaking as an American company or British English?
And then I started doing the style guides, the editorial standards.
I created a bunch of checklists as well for each of the contributors.
So the piece, you need to pass, pass, pass this level before you even send it to me as the head of editorial.
Like, I don't want to start doing line editing for a piece, because we're actually publishing according to a schedule.
Then I set up our asana board.
Before now, there was no asana, everything was managed via email.
And then I set up the asana board for using a Kanban flow, where we actually have assigned, ideating, drafting, delivered, under review, published, live, and with the links and all of that.
We implemented the HRF so that we can actually download and see the performance of the pieces, who is reading it and all of that.
connecting that back into the marketing workflow as well when the marketing team is trying to promote the pieces and all of that.
The other thing I did was also started using AI in that workflow because I think about in terms of editing, I think AI has actually gotten pretty good in terms of line editing.
Like, do you have the full stops or the comma or semicolon?
I actually don't think it's efficient to have a human doing all of that.
You can't do that at scale.
So I built this style guide into Grammarly and all the writers that are writing for Criptonary were signing to that Grammarly account.
So as soon as you're signing to that Grammarly account and you're typing on Google Docs, automatically it just does the line edit from that point on.
So I used that to cut off the manual editorial point of having everything going to that way because we had a single editor and it was like a choke point from my own perspective.
Yeah, yeah.
And then so the only thing the editor then had to look at was then polish for style or sophistication rather than checking if there's a comma somewhere.
time.
title.
You
So that was one of the things I did to actually streamline the workflow process.

Then something else I did was in terms of when I'm looking at pieces myself, if I have to correct a piece first time, second time, if it's the same issue the third time, I'm shooting a Loom video talking about why this is wrong, how I fixed it, how it should be addressed in future, I pinned that in the Slack channel and all of that, ensuring that everybody has access to it.
And of Slack, I also deferred to saying, let's have our communications in the channel, not in EMS.
So I have visibility into what's going on.

I have visibility into what's going on across board, because one thing I've observed is sometimes two analysts are talking to each other about the project, but the rest of the company doesn't know about it.
And I'm like, and then they want to publish an analysis.
And that's the first time the head of analysis is saying it.
And it's like, but this analysis doesn't make sense.
And I'm like, how don't you know?
And it's like, well, I didn't know when they were discussing it.
And so everything has to happen in the channel, so there's visibility.
So those are some of the specific steps.
So to like put a structure around how we actually publish a cryptonary, and it worked.
When I started, it was like 20,000 users to begin with.
mean, paying users, they actually had like a repertoire of like 120,000 users, but they're actually paying users for like 20,000.
And in over seven months, we've moved to like close to 45,000, especially at the peak of that bull run.
Wow.
Yeah.
Wow.
I mean, I also instituted some location-based pricing, which also helped a lot of that.
I wouldn't say the editorial function itself was the only thing that led to that growth.
But people had more confidence, like Monday is market direction, Tuesday is market updates, Wednesday is a live stream, Thursday you have the end drops.
People know what they can expect on a consistent basis.
So people had more confidence to pay for the service.
Yeah.

I'd love to dive in more into your AI experience.
So you mentioned Grammarly and building like the writing guidelines into.
How else have you used AI in generating content?
So, I think it actually started off back from animals.
animals, experimented with Jasper.
That was when Jasper started.
Now, Jasper is more like a fuel suit AI marketing tool.
But when it started, it was really like to draft pieces.
And we experimented with writing pieces in Jasper.
And then, you know, chat, GPT, Claude.
So, the way I think about AI is like, I think about it in terms of when I describe it to my friends, a armor and a power drill, even a carpenter, both will do the work.
But the guy with the power drill will do it faster, more precisely, more efficiently than the guy with the armor.
And so, if you are using work in this age without AI, it feels like you are still using a armor.
But you can't hand over your thinking, your reasoning to the AI model.
So, it's like you still need that human in the loop.
So, in my experience, I've used Claude and ChatGPT, Ideation.
Coming up with around the first draft and all of that, and then I'm refining the thought processes for that specific drafting.
Even in SEO, use ClearScope specifically to see what are the competitors rating, how can we write something that's actually better than what they've written, and have a shot at winning it.
So when we are writing an SEO piece, you are not shooting blindly.
We know specifically what we are writing and how we need to film it, even the number of words, because ClearScope actually ran the pieces in terms of A plus B and all of that.
And we know what we need to do to get an A plus to win.
So that's in terms of AI.
Over the last two months, I've also been building something I call OnBrand.
It's currently a command line tool, but maybe sometime in the interview process, or maybe if I come on board, I can show you how it was.
But it's basically enforcing brand consistency.
So what I designed is really, it's really more like prompt engineering in a cloud wrapper.
But basically, you paste your copy, and then I've designed some metrics in the code along the spectrum of people.
If you're serious, former, informal, technical, non-technical, and then you design what the brand voice should sound like.
And it takes every piece of content you post and analyzes it against that brand voice and tell you whether you are sounding on brand or off-brand.
And I think this is something that when companies have multiple touch points of creating content, they can use to actually enforce brand consistency.
And that's something I'm building myself using Python.
I actually have it in my GitHub, so it's publicly available, but you need to download four kits and use that as a command line.
So, I mean, if I have time, I might actually build a front-end application for it, but right now the code is fully functional, four-kable on GitHub, and people can use it.

you have thoughts on whether there's a step missing or what checks or balances or sources that we should bring in for the workflow to the AI to check, right?
